section.title: The Two Eagles and the Vine
section.sectionRationale: Ezekiel is told to pose a riddle rather than simply announce judgment: a great eagle plants a vine that then bends toward a second eagle for water, a veiled picture of King Zedekiah's sworn covenant with Babylon and his treacherous reach for help from Egypt. The riddle is unlocked in plain language, judgment is pronounced, and then the chapter turns unexpectedly toward hope: God Himself will plant a tender shoot that grows into a sheltering, messianic cedar.
section.thesis: God exposes Zedekiah's broken oath to Babylon as treason against Himself, since the covenant had been sworn in His name, and though the failed king's own scheming brings him to ruin, the LORD promises to plant His own tender shoot on Zion, a kingdom that will grow from humble beginnings into a shelter for every nation, fulfilled in Christ.
section.frameNote: Four movements: a great eagle plants a low, dependent vine; a second eagle to whom the vine treacherously turns for water; the riddle unlocked as Zedekiah's broken covenant with Babylon and his betrayal to Egypt; and God's own counter-promise, a tender shoot planted into a majestic, messianic cedar.

units.label: A great eagle plants a vine

verses.text: Now the word of the LORD came to me, saying,
“Son of man, pose a {{chidah|riddle}}; speak a parable to the house of Israel
and tell them that this is what the Lord GOD says: ‘A great {{nesher|eagle}} with great wings and long pinions, full of feathers of many colors, came to Lebanon and took away the top of the cedar.
He plucked off its topmost shoot, carried it to the land of merchants, and planted it in a city of traders.
He took some of the seed of the land and planted it in fertile soil; he placed it by abundant waters and set it out like a willow.
It sprouted and became a spreading vine, low in height, with branches turned toward him; yet its roots remained where it stood. So it became a vine and yielded branches and sent out shoots.

units.label: A second eagle, and a vine that turns
units.note: the vine bends toward Egypt instead of trusting its planter

verses.text: But there was another great eagle with great wings and many feathers. And behold, this vine bent its roots toward him. It stretched out its branches to him from its planting bed, so that he might water it.
It had been planted in good soil by abundant waters in order to yield branches and bear fruit and become a splendid vine.’

So you are to tell them that this is what the Lord GOD says: ‘Will it flourish? Will it not be uprooted and stripped of its fruit so that it shrivels? All its foliage will wither! It will not take a strong arm or many people to pull it up by its roots.
Even if it is transplanted, will it flourish? Will it not completely wither when the east wind strikes? It will wither on the bed where it sprouted.’”

units.label: The riddle unlocked, a broken oath
units.note: Zedekiah's covenant with Babylon and betrayal to Egypt

verses.text: Then the word of the LORD came to me, saying,

“Now say to this rebellious house: ‘Do you not know what these things mean?’ Tell them, ‘Behold, the king of Babylon came to Jerusalem, carried off its king and officials, and brought them back with him to Babylon.
He took a member of the royal family and made a {{berit|covenant}} with him, putting him under oath. Then he carried away the leading men of the land,
so that the kingdom would be brought low, unable to lift itself up, surviving only by keeping his covenant.

But this king rebelled against Babylon by sending his envoys to Egypt to ask for horses and a large army. Will he flourish? Will the one who does such things escape? Can he break a covenant and yet escape?’
‘As surely as I live,’ declares the Lord GOD, ‘he will die in Babylon, in the land of the king who enthroned him, whose oath he despised and whose covenant he broke.
Pharaoh with his mighty army and vast horde will not help him in battle, when ramps are built and siege walls constructed to destroy many lives.
He despised the oath by breaking the covenant. Seeing that he gave his hand in pledge yet did all these things, he will not escape!’
Therefore this is what the Lord GOD says: ‘As surely as I live, I will bring down upon his head My oath that he despised and My covenant that he broke.
I will spread My net over him and catch him in My snare. I will bring him to Babylon and execute judgment upon him there for the treason he committed against Me.
All his choice troops will fall by the sword, and those who survive will be scattered to every wind. Then you will know that I, the LORD, have spoken.’

units.label: The tender shoot of the LORD's planting
units.note: a messianic promise of a kingdom to come

verses.text: This is what the Lord GOD says: ‘I will take a shoot from the lofty top of the cedar, and I will set it out. I will pluck a {{yoneqet|tender sprig}} from its topmost shoots, and I will plant it on a high and lofty mountain.

I will plant it on the mountain heights of Israel so that it will bear branches; it will yield fruit and become a majestic cedar. Birds of every kind will nest under it, taking shelter in the shade of its branches.

Then all the trees of the field will know that I am the LORD. I bring the tall tree down and make the low tree tall. I dry up the green tree and make the withered tree flourish. I, the LORD, have spoken, and I have done it.’”

chidah.gloss: A riddle or dark saying, a puzzle to be pondered before its meaning is unlocked. Ezekiel is told to pose a riddle rather than state Judah's judgment plainly, so that the house of Israel must reckon with the truth for themselves before it is spelled out in verses 11 to 21.

nesher.gloss: Eagle, a common ancient image for a great king or empire swooping down in conquest. The first eagle pictures Nebuchadnezzar of Babylon; the second, introduced in verse 7, pictures Pharaoh of Egypt, to whom Judah foolishly turned instead of trusting the covenant it had sworn.

berit.gloss: Covenant, here the vassal treaty Zedekiah swore to Nebuchadnezzar in the LORD's name, 2 Chronicles 36:13. Breaking it was not merely political treachery but, in Ezekiel's theology, treason against God Himself, since the oath had been sworn by His name.

yoneqet.gloss: A tender shoot or young twig, easily overlooked beside the 'lofty top' from which it is taken. God's promised king grows not from human scheming, like Zedekiah's failed diplomacy, but from God's own quiet planting, a pattern Isaiah and the Gospels apply to the Messiah's humble, surprising rise.

basic.context: This chapter follows Ezekiel's sign-acts and oracles of judgment with a riddle-parable aimed at 'this rebellious house'. Judah's king Zedekiah, installed as Babylon's vassal and bound by a sworn covenant, secretly appealed to Egypt for military help, breaking his oath. The parable of two eagles and a vine exposes this betrayal, and ends with a surprising counter-promise: God Himself will plant a tender shoot that grows into a sheltering, majestic cedar.
basic.hermeneutics: This is a riddle, a mashal, deliberately puzzling until Ezekiel unlocks it himself in verses 11 to 21: the first eagle is Nebuchadnezzar, the vine is Zedekiah, the second eagle is Egypt. The historical betrayal is real and the judgment concrete, but the closing image of the tender shoot reaches beyond the immediate political crisis to a promise that outlasts it.
basic.originalLanguages: Ezekiel poses a chidah, a riddle, whose two nesher, eagles, and bent vine picture Judah's fatal reach for Egypt after swearing a berit, covenant, to Babylon in the LORD's name. The chapter turns on a yoneqet, a tender and easily overlooked shoot, which God Himself will plant into a majestic, sheltering cedar.
basic.typology: The tender shoot planted on a high mountain that grows into a cedar sheltering birds of every kind anticipates Christ's kingdom: small and unimpressive in its beginnings, as in the parable of the mustard seed, yet becoming a refuge for every nation.
basic.christ: Ezekiel 17:22-24 promises that God, not human scheming, will plant a tender shoot from the cedar's lofty top onto Israel's mountain heights, where it becomes a majestic tree sheltering birds of every kind. This is echoed in Isaiah's shoot from the stump of Jesse, 11:1, Jeremiah's righteous Branch, 23:5, and Jesus' parable of the mustard seed becoming a tree in whose branches birds can nest, Matthew 13:31-32, His kingdom growing from humble, hidden beginnings into a shelter for every people.
basic.god: God exposes political treachery for what it is, treason against Himself, since Zedekiah's broken treaty had been sworn in the LORD's name; His justice topples the proud schemer who trusted Egypt over his own sworn word. Yet the same God who judges Zedekiah's failed self-rescue announces His own rescue plan, planting a king by His own hand rather than leaving the Davidic line's future to human intrigue.
basic.crossReferences: Isaiah 11:1-10 promises a shoot from Jesse's stump bearing the Spirit's fullness. Jeremiah 23:5-6 promises a righteous Branch to reign as David's true heir. Matthew 13:31-32 pictures the kingdom of heaven as a mustard seed growing into a tree where birds nest, echoing this chapter's image directly. 2 Kings 24:17-20 and 2 Chronicles 36:11-13 record Zedekiah's historical installation and broken oath.
basic.oneStory: This is the story of a king who broke his sworn word chasing a human rescue that could never hold, and the God who answers not with abandonment but with His own planting, a tender shoot who becomes a sheltering kingdom, tracing a straight line to Jesus, the Branch in whom every nation finds a home.

context.literary: A riddle-parable, a mashal, in two movements, the vision of two eagles and a vine, and its plain-spoken decoding, followed by a short oracle of promise; the pattern of veiled image followed by direct explanation runs throughout Ezekiel's sign-acts and visions.
context.historical: Delivered while Zedekiah, Nebuchadnezzar's own vassal-king, was secretly negotiating with Egypt for military aid against Babylon, in direct violation of a treaty sworn in the LORD's name, this oracle predicts, accurately, his capture and death in Babylon, fulfilled at Jerusalem's fall in 586 BC, 2 Kings 25:1-7.
context.canonical: Belongs to the prophetic tradition of tree and kingdom imagery running from Daniel's vision of a great tree representing a kingdom, Daniel 4, through Isaiah's shoot from Jesse, to Jesus' mustard seed parable, all describing kingdoms rising, falling, or planted by God's own hand.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To expose Zedekiah's covenant treachery as treason against the LORD Himself, whose name sealed the broken oath, while promising that Judah's political and dynastic collapse is not God's last word: He will yet plant His own king.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The riddle describes a specific historical betrayal and its consequences; it is not a manual for statecraft. Its abiding truth is that oaths sworn in God's name matter to Him, and that God's redemptive purposes are never thwarted by human political failure.

debates.question: Does the tender shoot planted on the mountain, 17:22-24, refer only to a near-term restoration of the Davidic line, or does it point ultimately to the Messiah?

views.label: Primarily a near-term restoration
views.case: The image describes the humbled Davidic line eventually reestablished in the land after exile, a real, historical hope for Ezekiel's first hearers awaiting return from Babylon.

views.label: Ultimately messianic, fulfilled in Christ
views.case: The language of a majestic cedar under which 'birds of every kind will nest' echoes messianic kingdom imagery, and no merely postexilic restoration produced an everlasting, universal kingdom, so the promise reaches its full sense only in Christ.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree God Himself, not human politics, secures the future of David's line; the near-term hope of restoration anticipates and is not exhausted until its true and lasting fulfillment in the kingdom of Jesus, the Messiah.

study.originalLanguages: Ezekiel poses a chidah, a riddle, whose two nesher, eagles, and bent vine picture Judah's fatal reach for Egypt after swearing a berit, covenant, to Babylon in the LORD's name. The chapter turns on a yoneqet, a tender and easily overlooked shoot, which God Himself will plant into a majestic, sheltering cedar, fulfilled at last in Christ.

typology.type: The tender shoot planted on Israel's mountain heights, growing into a majestic, sheltering cedar, Ezekiel 17:22-24
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled in Christ's kingdom, growing from humble beginnings, as in the parable of the mustard seed, Matthew 13:31-32, into a shelter for every nation.

typology.type: The proud tree brought down and the low tree made tall, Ezekiel 17:24
typology.fulfillment: Echoed in Christ's own teaching and pattern of humiliation and exaltation, Philippians 2:8-9, Luke 1:52.

study.christ: Ezekiel 17:22-24 promises that God, not human scheming, will plant a tender shoot from the cedar's lofty top onto Israel's mountain heights, where it becomes a majestic tree sheltering birds of every kind. This is echoed in Isaiah's shoot from the stump of Jesse, 11:1-10, Jeremiah's righteous Branch, 23:5-6, and Jesus' own parable of the mustard seed becoming a tree in whose branches birds can nest, Matthew 13:31-32, His kingdom growing from humble, hidden beginnings into a shelter for every people and nation.
study.god: God exposes political treachery for what it truly is, treason against Himself, since Zedekiah's broken treaty had been sworn in the LORD's own name; His justice brings down the proud schemer who trusted Egypt over his sworn word. Yet the very God who judges Zedekiah's failed self-rescue announces His own rescue plan, planting a king by His own hand rather than leaving the future of David's line to human intrigue or failed alliances.

crossReferences.note: Records the historical installation of Zedekiah as Babylon's vassal king and his later rebellion.
Promises a shoot from the stump of Jesse on whom the Spirit of the LORD will rest.
Promises a righteous Branch to reign wisely as David's true heir.
Jesus' parable of the mustard seed becoming a tree where birds nest directly echoes this chapter's closing image.

study.oneStory: This is the story of a king who broke his sworn word chasing a human rescue that could never hold, and the God who answers not with abandonment but with His own planting, a tender shoot who becomes a sheltering kingdom, tracing a straight line to Jesus, the Branch in whom every nation finds a home.

quiz.q: What does the first eagle do to the cedar in Ezekiel's riddle, and what does it represent?

options.text: It destroys the whole cedar, leaving nothing standing.
options.note: Only the topmost shoot is plucked away, not the whole tree; the picture is deportation of the royal house, not total destruction.

options.text: It plucks off the cedar's topmost shoot and plants it in a city of traders, picturing Nebuchadnezzar carrying off Judah's king to Babylon.
options.note: Verses 3-4 and 12 confirm this exactly, the first eagle acting for Babylon.

options.text: It waters the cedar so it grows taller than before.
options.note: The eagle removes the shoot and carries it away; it does not nourish the cedar in place.

options.text: It ignores the cedar entirely and flies elsewhere.
options.note: The eagle is deeply involved, plucking and transplanting the shoot; it does not merely pass by.

quiz.q: What does the transplanted vine do when a second eagle appears?

options.text: It stays rooted only toward the one who first planted it, refusing the second eagle.
options.note: The opposite happens; the vine bends its roots and branches toward the second eagle.

options.text: It bends its roots and stretches its branches toward the second eagle, seeking water it did not need.
options.note: Verse 7 states this plainly, even though verse 8 notes it was already planted by abundant waters.

options.text: It withers immediately without any explanation given.
options.note: Withering is announced only later, in verses 9-10, as the coming judgment for this disloyalty.

options.text: It grows a little toward the second eagle but mostly remains unaffected.
options.note: The text describes a decisive, full turning of roots and branches, not a slight or partial shift.

quiz.q: According to the riddle's explanation in verses 12-15, what did Judah's king do that provoked judgment?

options.text: He kept his sworn covenant with Babylon faithfully throughout his reign.
options.note: The text says the opposite; he despised the oath and broke the covenant.

options.text: He simply grew old and died a natural death in Jerusalem.
options.note: Verse 16 specifies he would die in Babylon, not naturally in Jerusalem, as judgment for his betrayal.

options.text: He rebelled against Babylon by sending envoys to Egypt for horses and a large army, breaking his sworn covenant.
options.note: Verse 15 states this directly, and verses 18-19 identify it as breaking a covenant sworn in the LORD's name.

options.text: He rebelled against Babylon but never sought any foreign alliance.
options.note: His rebellion specifically involved seeking Egypt's military help, not standing alone.

quiz.q: Why does God treat the breaking of Zedekiah's political treaty with Babylon as treason against Himself?

options.text: Because political treaties always carry automatic religious weight regardless of how they are sworn.
options.note: The text specifies the reason as the oath being sworn in the LORD's name, not treaties in general.

options.text: Because the treaty happened to be signed inside the temple courts.
options.note: No such detail about the temple appears in this chapter's account of the covenant.

options.text: Because the covenant and oath had been sworn in the LORD's own name, so breaking it despised Him personally.
options.note: Verses 18-19 make this explicit: 'My oath that he despised and My covenant that he broke.'

options.text: Because Babylon was itself a worshiper of the LORD and deserved special loyalty.
options.note: Babylon's own religious status is not the point; the oath's connection to the LORD's name is what makes betrayal treasonous.

quiz.q: What does God promise to do in the chapter's closing verses, 17:22-24?

options.text: Leave the Davidic line permanently ended with no future planting or restoration.
options.note: The chapter's very point is a promised future planting, the opposite of permanent ending.

options.text: Allow human political alliances to eventually restore the kingdom on their own.
options.note: The chapter has just shown such alliances failing catastrophically; the promise is explicitly God's own planting, not human diplomacy.

options.text: Restore the kingdom, but only as a low vine like the one already judged.
options.note: The image escalates beyond a low, dependent vine to a majestic, towering cedar sheltering many nations.

options.text: Plant a tender shoot on a high mountain that grows into a majestic cedar sheltering birds of every kind.
options.note: Verses 22-23 state this promise directly, God's own sovereign planting rather than human scheming.

questions: Zedekiah broke a sworn covenant for the sake of a political safety net in Egypt. Where might you be tempted to break a promise to God because a 'safer' backup plan looks more secure?
The vine bent itself toward a second source of water even though it was already planted by abundant waters. What 'second waters' are you quietly leaning on instead of trusting God's provision?
God plants His own tender shoot rather than depending on human scheming to secure a king. Where do you need to trust God's timing rather than anxiously engineering your own rescue?
The chapter ends with an invitation: 'Birds of every kind will nest under it.' How does knowing Christ's kingdom welcomes every people shape how you view those who seem furthest from it?
God says, 'I bring the tall tree down and make the low tree tall.' Where have you seen God overturn merely human measures of importance and status in your own story?

translationNotes.note: The two eagles are not named in the riddle itself; verses 12-17 identify the first with Babylon and, by implication, the second with Egypt, whose horses and army Zedekiah sought, verse 15.

'Made a covenant with him, putting him under oath' reflects the historical account in 2 Chronicles 36:13, where Zedekiah is said to have rebelled against Nebuchadnezzar 'though he had made him swear allegiance by God.'

'Tender sprig' translates a Hebrew word for a young, easily overlooked twig, deliberately contrasted with the 'lofty top' from which it is taken, underscoring the humble beginnings of the promised kingdom.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session via the makor scripture toolkit for Ezekiel 17:1-24. Minor embedded line breaks within verses 3, 4, 6, 7, 9, 12, and 22 were flattened into continuous prose; no em or en dashes were present in the source verses. No sermon from the trusted cluster was found specifically scoped to Ezekiel 17, so sermon status is none.
